variable.description: Paste the token returned by Login here.

info.name: JussiLog API Documentation
info.description: REST API documentation for Jussilog endpoints.

item.name: Ask a question about the authenticated user's primary resume.

body.raw: {"question":"What are the main skills on this resume?","language":"fi"}

response.body: {"message": "No primary resume found for this user."}

item.name: Regenerate thumbnail images for products and/or resumes.

query.description: Which model to process: products, resumes, or all (default).

item.name: Delete all thumbnail images for products and/or resumes (originals are kept).

query.description: Which model to purge: products, resumes, or all (default).

{"current_page":1,"data":[{"id":4,"user_id":1,"blog_category_id":1,"title":"Welcome to the Blog","slug":"welcome-to-the-blog","excerpt":"This is the first blog post.","content":"

Welcome! This is an example blog post created by the seeder.<\/p>","featured_image":null,"featured_image_sizes":null,"tags":null,"visibility":true,"created_at":"2026-03-19T11:56:10.000000Z","updated_at":"2026-03-19T11:56:10.000000Z","author":{"id":1,"first_name":"Super","last_name":"Admin","name":"superadmin"},"category":{"id":1,"name":"Default","slug":"default"}},{"id":3,"user_id":1,"blog_category_id":1,"title":"sdasdsadasdsa","slug":"sdasdsadasdsa","excerpt":"dasdasdas","content":"

Until recently, the prevailing view assumed lorem ipsum<\/em> was born as a nonsense text. \u201cIt\u2019s not Latin, though it looks like it, and it actually says nothing,\u201d Before & After magazine answered a curious reader<\/a>, \u201cIts \u2018words\u2019 loosely approximate the frequency with which letters occur in English, which is why at a glance it looks pretty real.\u201d<\/p>

As Cicero would put it, \u201cUm, not so fast.\u201d<\/p>

The placeholder text, beginning with the line \u201cLorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit\u201d, looks like Latin because in its youth, centuries ago, it was Latin.<\/p>

Richard McClintock, a Latin scholar from Hampden-Sydney College, is credited<\/a> with discovering the source behind the ubiquitous filler text. In seeing a sample of lorem ipsum, his interest was piqued by consectetur\u2014a genuine, albeit rare, Latin word. Consulting a Latin dictionary led McClintock to a passage from De Finibus Bonorum et Malorum (\u201cOn the Extremes of Good and Evil\u201d), a first-century B.C. text from the Roman philosopher Cicero.<\/p>

In particular, the garbled words of lorem ipsum<\/em> bear an unmistakable resemblance to sections 1.10.32\u201333 of Cicero\u2019s work, with the most notable passage excerpted below:<\/p>

\u201cNeque porro quisquam est, qui dolorem ipsum quia dolor sit amet, consectetur, adipisci velit, sed quia non numquam eius modi tempora incidunt ut labore et dolore magnam aliquam quaerat voluptatem.\u201d<\/p><\/blockquote>

A 1914 English translation by Harris Rackham<\/a> reads:<\/p>

\u201cNor is there anyone who loves or pursues or desires to obtain pain of itself, because it is pain, but occasionally circumstances occur in which toil and pain can procure him some great pleasure.\u201d<\/p><\/blockquote>

McClintock\u2019s eye for detail certainly helped narrow the whereabouts of lorem ipsum\u2019s origin, however, the \u201chow and when\u201d still remain something of a mystery, with competing theories and timelines.<\/p>","featured_image":"blogs\/3\/featured_image_1773787684.jpg","featured_image_sizes":{"large":"blogs\/3\/1773787684_large.jpg","thumb":"blogs\/3\/1773787684_thumb.jpg","medium":"blogs\/3\/1773787684_medium.jpg"},"tags":["asd"],"visibility":true,"created_at":"2026-03-17T15:11:50.000000Z","updated_at":"2026-03-17T22:56:40.000000Z","author":{"id":1,"first_name":"Super","last_name":"Admin","name":"superadmin"},"category":{"id":1,"name":"Default","slug":"default"}}],"first_page_url":"http:\/\/localhost:8000\/api\/blogs?page=1","from":1,"last_page":1,"last_page_url":"http:\/\/localhost:8000\/api\/blogs?page=1","links":[{"url":null,"label":"« Previous","active":false},{"url":"http:\/\/localhost:8000\/api\/blogs?page=1","label":"1","active":true},{"url":null,"label":"Next »","active":false}],"next_page_url":null,"path":"http:\/\/localhost:8000\/api\/blogs","per_page":15,"prev_page_url":null,"to":2,"total":2}

Until recently, the prevailing view assumed lorem ipsum<\/em> was born as a nonsense text. \u201cIt\u2019s not Latin, though it looks like it, and it actually says nothing,\u201d Before & After magazine answered a curious reader<\/a>, \u201cIts \u2018words\u2019 loosely approximate the frequency with which letters occur in English, which is why at a glance it looks pretty real.\u201d<\/p>

As Cicero would put it, \u201cUm, not so fast.\u201d<\/p>

The placeholder text, beginning with the line \u201cLorem ipsum dolor sit amet, consectetur adipiscing elit\u201d, looks like Latin because in its youth, centuries ago, it was Latin.<\/p>

Richard McClintock, a Latin scholar from Hampden-Sydney College, is credited<\/a> with discovering the source behind the ubiquitous filler text. In seeing a sample of lorem ipsum, his interest was piqued by consectetur\u2014a genuine, albeit rare, Latin word. Consulting a Latin dictionary led McClintock to a passage from De Finibus Bonorum et Malorum (\u201cOn the Extremes of Good and Evil\u201d), a first-century B.C. text from the Roman philosopher Cicero.<\/p>

In particular, the garbled words of lorem ipsum<\/em> bear an unmistakable resemblance to sections 1.10.32\u201333 of Cicero\u2019s work, with the most notable passage excerpted below:<\/p>

\u201cNeque porro quisquam est, qui dolorem ipsum quia dolor sit amet, consectetur, adipisci velit, sed quia non numquam eius modi tempora incidunt ut labore et dolore magnam aliquam quaerat voluptatem.\u201d<\/p><\/blockquote>

item.name: List invoices for the authenticated user.

query.description: Filter by status (draft, issued, paid, cancelled).
Sort field (id, invoice_number, subtotal, total, status, issued_at, paid_at, created_at).

request.description: Returns a paginated list of invoices belonging to the currently authenticated user.

item.name: Return available invoice statuses and item types with translated labels.

query.description: Language code (en, fi). Defaults to en.

item.name: Export a preview invoice as PDF (no auth, no database save).

request.description: Builds a transient invoice from the request body and returns it as a downloadable PDF.

item.name: Export a preview invoice as HTML (no auth, no database save).

request.description: Builds a transient invoice from the request body and returns it as an HTML file.

item.name: Send a preview invoice by email (no auth, no database save).

request.description:
Builds a transient invoice from the request body and sends it to `to_email`.
If `to_email` is omitted, falls back to `customer_email` in the payload.
Sends the invoice as a PDF attachment to the customer email or to a specific email address if provided.

response.body: {"message": "The email field must be a valid email address.", "errors": {}}
response.name: Validation error

query.description: Filter by status (draft, issued, paid, cancelled).
Sort field (id, invoice_number, subtotal, total, status, issued_at, paid_at, created_at).

request.description: Returns a paginated list of invoices. Results can be filtered by order, user and status.
Returns a single invoice with its items, associated order and user.
Returns a PDF file of the invoice.

item.name: Download a stored invoice as HTML.

request.description: Creates a new invoice for the given order and automatically generates invoice items from the order's line items.

response.name: Validation error

request.description: Updates an existing invoice. Automatically sets `issued_at` when status changes to `issued`, and `paid_at` when status changes to `paid`.

response.name: Validation error

request.description: Permanently deletes an invoice and all its associated items.

item.name: Export a resume as a PDF from a JSON payload (no stored resume required).

request.description:
The photo can be supplied as a base64-encoded string in `photo`.
The result is identical to the authenticated export but nothing is written to the database.

item.name: Export a resume as an HTML file from a JSON payload (no stored resume required).

request.description: The photo can be supplied as a base64-encoded string in `photo`.

item.name: Preview a resume as an inline PDF from a JSON payload (no stored resume required).

request.description:
The photo can be supplied as a base64-encoded string in `photo`.
Returns the PDF inline (for iframe / browser preview), not as a download.

item.name: Preview a resume as inline HTML from a JSON payload (no stored resume required).

request.description:
The photo can be supplied as a base64-encoded string in `photo`.
Returns HTML inline (for iframe / browser preview), not as a download.

query.description: Username of the resume owner (required for public access).
Resume access code (required when the resume has one).

request.description:
Accessible either with a valid Sanctum token (returns the authenticated
user's primary resume) or without a token by supplying `owner` (username)
query parameter; if the resume has a code set, `code` must also match.

item.name: Get the main (primary) resume for public display.

query.description: Access code (required when the resume has one).

request.description:
The resume must be set to public (`is_public = true`).
Supports two lookup modes:
- By resume ID (`?id=<n>`)
- By owner username (`?owner=<username>`)

If the resume has a code set, the `code` query parameter must match.

item.name: Display a specific public resume (no token required).

query.description: Access code (required when the resume has one).

request.description:
The resume must be set to public (`is_public = true`).
If the resume has a code set, the `code` query parameter must match.

item.name: Display all resumes for the authenticated user.

query.description: Number of results per page (1-100). Defaults to 10.
Field to sort by (id, title, created_at, updated_at). Defaults to updated_at.
Sort direction (asc, desc). Defaults to desc.

item.name: Return a single country by its ISO 3166-1 alpha-2 code.

item.name: Return all available tax rates with translated country names.

request.description:
Admins can delete any user. A regular user can only delete their own account
and must confirm with their current password (not required for OAuth-only accounts).
